title: Memory Hierarchy in Computer Systems
examTip: Memory hierarchy: Registers → L1 Cache → L2 Cache → L3 Cache → RAM → SSD/HDD → Cloud/Tape. Going down: larger capacity, slower speed, lower cost per bit. Locality of Reference: Temporal (recently used data reused) and Spatial (nearby data also needed).

layers.name: CPU Registers
layers.protocols: Size: 32–512 bytes  |  Speed: 0-1 ns  |  Cost: $$$$$  |  Location: Inside CPU

layers.name: L1 Cache
layers.protocols: Size: 32–64 KB  |  Speed: 1–4 ns  |  Cost: $$$$  |  Location: On-chip (per core)

layers.name: L2 Cache
layers.protocols: Size: 256 KB–1 MB  |  Speed: 4–12 ns  |  Cost: $$$  |  Location: On-chip or near-chip

layers.name: L3 Cache (LLC)
layers.protocols: Size: 4–64 MB  |  Speed: 12–40 ns  |  Cost: $$  |  Location: On-chip (shared)

layers.name: Main Memory (RAM)
layers.protocols: Size: 4–512 GB  |  Speed: 50–100 ns  |  Cost: $  |  Volatile: Yes

layers.name: Secondary Storage
layers.protocols: Size: 256 GB–20 TB  |  Speed: 0.1–10 ms  |  Cost: ¢  |  Volatile: No

layers.name: Tertiary / Offline
layers.protocols: Size: TB–PB  |  Speed: seconds to minutes  |  Cost: fractions of ¢  |  Permanent

theory:
The Memory Hierarchy is a structured organization of storage systems in a computer, arranged by speed, cost, and capacity. The fundamental trade-off is: faster memory costs more per bit and has smaller capacity. The hierarchy exploits the principle of Locality of Reference to provide the illusion of large, fast, cheap memory.

Locality of Reference: Temporal Locality — recently accessed data will likely be accessed again soon (loop variables). Spatial Locality — data near recently accessed locations will likely be accessed soon (array traversal).

CPU Registers: The fastest storage, directly accessed by the CPU in 0–1 clock cycles. Holds the operands for currently executing instructions. Typically 32 to 64 registers of 64-bit width.

L1 Cache: Small (32–64 KB), extremely fast (1–4 ns), per-core cache. Instructions and data are split into separate L1-I and L1-D caches. Uses SRAM (Static RAM) which is faster but more expensive than DRAM.

L2 Cache: Larger (256 KB–1 MB), slightly slower (4–12 ns). May be unified or split. Still SRAM, still on-chip.

L3 Cache (Last Level Cache — LLC): Largest on-chip cache (4–64 MB), shared among all cores. Acts as last resort before going to main memory.

Main Memory (RAM): DRAM (Dynamic RAM) technology. Volatile (loses data when power removed). Much larger capacity than cache. Virtual memory system manages RAM allocation.

Secondary Storage: Non-volatile persistent storage. SSDs use NAND flash memory; HDDs use magnetic spinning disks. Data organized in blocks and sectors.

Tertiary Storage: Magnetic tape for long-term archival; cloud storage for remote backup. Extremely high capacity at minimal cost, but very high access latency.
keyPoints: 7 levels: Registers → L1 → L2 → L3 → RAM → Secondary Storage → Tertiary Storage
Going down: larger capacity, slower speed, lower cost per bit
Temporal locality: recently used data reused; Spatial locality: nearby data also used
Cache hit: data found in cache (fast); Cache miss: must fetch from lower level (slow)
RAM is volatile (DRAM); Secondary/Tertiary storage is non-volatile (persists without power)
useCases: CPU cache design — L1/L2/L3 cache sizing decisions in Intel, AMD, ARM processors
OS virtual memory management — page replacement algorithms (LRU, FIFO, Optimal)
Database buffer pool management — keeping hot data in RAM to avoid disk I/O
